mohammed kamaluddin sayyed saidu vs state by puttur rural police on march, . it is the case of the prosecution that, the accused no. is the resident of karkalli, bane house of nagarror village of somawarpete taluk of kodagu district and married to the daughter of puthumonu, resident of kodankeri of kodimbala village, puttur taluk. accused no. is the resident of muriyala house of uruvalu village of belthangady taluk and married the daughter of mammunhi, resident of kodengeri of kodimbala village, puttur taluk, situated nearby the house of the wife of the accused no.. they came to know each other while coming to their in-laws house and became close to each other. accused no. earlier supplied laterite stone and sand to one baby thomas while constructing his house in sy.no. at undila of kodimbala village and had good relationship with him. accused no. shared this fact with accused no. and also disclosed that baby thomas and his wife mary thomas only two were residing in the house. at that time, accused no. shared his financial difficulties and hence, both in furtherance of their common intention of gaining money wrongfully, decided to rob the golden ornaments worn by baby thomas and his wife mary thomas. it is the further case of the prosecution that, on , they brought the motorcycle of accused no. from his house at somawarpete bearing no.ka..l. and while coming, they purchased two knives from the market of somawarpete and reached kodimbala at night . p.m. and by switching off the motorcycle, pulled the motorcycle near the house of baby thomas to park it there on the guise that motorcycle was out of order. then thomas in order to drop them to their in-laws house took them in his car bearing registration no.kl--t- .

. the complainant party and the accused party were the residents of village chhapar at the time of the occurrence. . deceased uttam singh and deceased pyari bai are the father and the mother of virendra singh pw and madhav singh pw respectively and deceased usha bai is the wife of said virendra singh. at the time of death of usha bai, she was carrying months old pregnancy. . the case of prosecution as unfolded before the trial court, in brief, is as under:- . on at about : pm in village chhapar, complainant virendra singh made an oral fir to rakesh tiwari pw, the sub- inspector and sho of police station satai, stating that in the morning of the same day at about : am, he and his brother madhav singh pw went to his agricultural field which is named as kolhu wala khet for irrigating the crops. they used a diesel engine for drawing water from the well. the irrigation of the crops was over at about : am. he, his father uttam singh since deceased and his brother madhav singh took the engine which is fitted on four wheeled platform by pulling and pushing to their another agricultural field, which is named as gadara wala kuan, through kachcha passage which passes through mer field ridge of the appellants. the appellants, who were present in their field, took objections regarding the carrying of engine through their mer. thereupon, they tendered apology and told them that they would take the engine back. meanwhile, appellants mangal singh and panjan singh with axes, appellant rampal singh with stick, appellant mankunwar bai with farsa and appellants sahodara bai, somwati bai and sadhna bai with sickles assaulted uttam singh.

the state vs . jasbir so randhir singh on december, the matrix of the facts, unfolded during the course of trial, which needs a sc no. state vs jasbir ors page of d.o.d p.s prashant vihar b , at about : am, the labours working at haiderpur water treatment plant noticed dead body of a male person lying in the water. they informed, then electrician shift incharge posted at said plant namely shambhu nath meena who is shown to have made a call to pcr. pcr police along with police officials from p.s prashant vihar reached there . dead body was taken out from the water and was removed to bjrm hospital for postmortem. the information about the aforesaid incident was recorded at p.s prashant vihar vide dd no. a, which was entrusted to some papers documents and visiting card were found in the pocket of the jacket worn by deceased, on the basis of which deceased was identified as devender so rajbir ro pithopra , distt sonepat, haryana. information was given to the brother of the deceased namely ravinder singh. dr. ashish jain conducted the autopsy on the dead body of deceased devender and opined the cause of death as cranio cerebral as a result of head injury produced by heavy sharp edged cutting weapon and time since death was days. after the postmortem, dead body was handed over to his relative.

on , at about : am raju pw- was returning from a public lavatory. he allegedly splashed water in front of ram parshads shop. the latter took offence to it. ram parshad abused on raju and chased him saying why he had thrown water there. raju got frightened and rushed into his house. he narrated ram parshads behaviour to his parents. rajus mother shanti devi the deceased suggested to his father ramji lal to call someone from the neighbourhood. it is alleged that while ramji lal had gone to call the neighbours, ram parshad reached rajus house armed with a churri. the appellant gave a lalkaara that he would not spare anyone on that day. the appellant gave a churri blow to rajus mothers left armpit. he raju raised an alarm and intervened to save his mother. at this, ram parshad gave a knife blow in rajus abdomen. in the meanwhile, his father ramji lal also reached home and tried to rescue raju. the appellant gave a churri blow on the left side of his fathers stomach. it is alleged that ram parshads mother batto devi also reached the spot armed with a danda, and gave a blow with it on rajus mothers head. ramji lal ram parshads father gave a lathi blow on rajus fathers right elbow. it is alleged that in the meanwhile, narayan and gopal reached the spot and rescued them. . in order to establish its case, the prosecution examined witnesses. . pw- raju, pw- ramji lal, pw- chet ram, and pw- kalyan singh are the material witnesses. . the trial court did not attach much importance to the discrepancies about apprehension of the culprit at the spot, discrepancy regarding duration of time between the death and the postmortem examination;

mukesh ahirwar vs the state of madhya pradesh on september, . the prosecution case, in brief, is that accused mukesh ahirwar used to teach sandhya raikwar pw. and her brothers and visit their house with effect from december,. as per prosecution case marriage of sandhya pw. was settled in village-navgaon, district-chhatarpur. however, it appears that sandhya pw. wrote letters article b and c to accused mukesh ahirwar. when her marriage was likely to be settled, she had disclosed the factum of writing of letters, to her father prakash raikwar. on when accused went to the house of sandhya, her mother krantibai pw. asked him to hand over the letters written by sandhya raikwar. accused asked sunil raikwar that he will give back the letters if he accompany him to his house. on that umesh raikwar pw. and sunil raikwar accompanied accused to his house. accused asked umesh raikwar pw. and sunil raikwar to sit outside and thereafter accused went inside the house and brought out the letters, but refused to hand over the letters to sunil raikwar, on that sunil raikwar made an effort to snatch the letters from him, on that accused with the help of gupti pointed sharp edged weapon inflicted injury on the head and abdomen of sunil raikwar , thereafter accused ran behind umesh raikwar along with gupti and chased him for yards. inspite of being injured ,sunil also ran towards that direction in order to save umesh raikwar, however, he fell down in front of grocery shop of keshram. on enquiry being made by surajkumar pw. and neetu pw., sunil raikwar made an oral dying declaration that accused had inflicted gupti injury on his person. from the pocket of deceased sunil letters article b and c were recovered.

allahabad high court shabbir khan vs mohd. ismail khan and ors. on october, equivalent citations: 1972crilj666 judgment s.d. khare, j. for himself and on behalf of m.n. shukla, j. . the main question to be considered by a learned single judge of this court was whether the offences under sections and . both read with i. p. c., could have been allowed to be compounded by the learned sessions judge, who had heard the against . . an offence under i. p. c. is compoundable, while an offence under i. p. c. is compoundable with the permission of the court vide cr. p. c.. the question for consideration is whether offences under sections and i. p. c. would be compoundable in the like manner. . according to the view taken by a division bench of this court in the case of state of u. p. v. chandrapal singh cri lj all. i. p. c. creates vicarious liability but does not create a distinct offence, and, therefore, an offence under i. p. c. is compoundable in the same manner as an offence under i. p. c. simpliciter. the learned single judge doubted the correctness of this decision because it has been held by the supreme court in a series of decisions that i. p. c. creates a distinct offence and therefore, a person charged for an offence under i. p. c. cannot be convicted under i. p. c. simpliciter if for one reason or another it is held that he was not a member of an unlawful assembly.

ram narain sharma vs canara bank on july, . concisely, case of the appellant is that he was appointed as a clerk in the canara bank on 19th may, and terms and conditions of his service were regulated by the canara bank service code. he was lastly appointed at kamla nagar branch, delhi. during his posting at kamla nagar branch, relations between him and the manager of the branch, namely, mr.b.p.rao became strained and the manager wanted his transfer but, when he failed in his efforts, he lodged a false complaint against the appellant with police on 4th july , making allegations of assault on him. appellant was accordingly charge sheeted in departmental enquiry but later on the enquiry was not proceeded with. criminal proceedings were lodged against the appellant for an offence under of the indian penal code hereinafter referred to as of the probation of offenders act hereinafter referred to as act. . succinctly, case of the respondent bank in defence is that victim mr.b.p.rao, the then manager of the branch was not happy with the working of the appellant and he had demanded appellants explanation for not doing his duties satisfactorily and carefully. appellant was also served with a memo demanding his explanation and thereafter manager had sent the report to his seniors about the callous work and conduct of the appellant. on 4th july, in between : am to : noon, appellant entered the office of the manager, abused him and asked him as to why he was harassing him. appellant thereafter lifted an iron rod and hit it on the head of the victim few times.

in the morning of , at about . am, the accused persons, sher mohd, firdos begum and sakila sakina trespassed into the house of the complainant, namely, surender singh. it is also alleged that all the accused persons wrongfully restrained the complainant and his wife, namely, smt. ram moorthi and beat them up, due to which they suffered simple injuries. it is further alleged that the complainant and his wife had got themselves medically examined and as per which, both had sustained simple injuries. . the learned predecessor of this court took cognizance upon the said police report on and issued summons to the accused persons. on appearance of accused persons, they were supplied with the copies of police report and documents. . ex. a- mlc no. of the complainant sh. surender singh chauhan dt. prepared by dr. nitin kumar, t.c. hospital. . the ld. counsel for the accused persons had argued that although the incident allegedly took place on but the complainant surender singh chauhan had made the complaint on and argued that this made the case of the prosecution doubtful. this submission made by ld. counsel for the accused persons is also devoid of merit. delay has the effect of putting the court on its guard to search if any explanation has been offered for the delay, and if offered, whether it is satisfactory or not. however, if the delay is explained to the satisfaction of the court, the delay cannot by itself be a ground for disbelieving and discarding the entire prosecution case. pw1complainant sh.

state vs parmod kamlani -:: page on september, . mr.parmod kamlani, the accused, has been charge sheeted by police station hari nagar, delhi for the offence under sections , and of the indian penal code hereinafter referred to as the - days prior to holi at about . noon at house no.m-, wz-, ii floor, hari nagar, delhi and thereafter - days after holi at the aforesaid place, he forcibly committed rape on the prosecutrix name withheld to protect her identity against her consent and threatened her to kill, if she raised alarm and he also gave beating to her. . as the prosecutrix was not certain who was the father of her child, the accused or mr.vijay bagi, her previous employer, mr.vijay bagi was kept as a suspect while the accused was chare sheeted and prosecuted by the prosecution. . the question is how to test the veracity of the prosecution story especially when it has some variations in the evidence. mere variance of the prosecution story with the evidence, in all cases, should not lead to the conclusion inevitably to reject the prosecution story. efforts should be made to find the truth, this is the very object for which the courts are created. to search it out, the courts have been removing chaff from the grain. it has to disperse the suspicious cloud and dust out the smear as all these things clog the very truth. so long chaff, cloud and dust remains, the criminals are clothed with this protective layer to receive the benefit of doubt. so it is a solemn duty of the courts, not to merely conclude and leave the case the moment suspicions are created.

anwar hussain vs the state of jharkhand on march, . the prosecution case as per the offence report is that on at about : p.m., when the forest personnels were on patrolling duty, they saw a truck coming in the village gangpur from gangpur - patthalgada road. when they intercepted the truck bearing registration no. bhm and examined it, they found the truck loaded with kendu leaves. the forest personnels demanded papers in respect of kendu leaves then driver handed over transport permit form n0. of bihar state forest development corporation limited sal biz project. the said transportation permit was of the year but the seal was of . dakpatti was also produced which was written on blank paper and in the aforesaid permit bags of kendu leaves were shown. on verification, it was found that the transportation permit presented by the driver was forged and fabricated because the permit presented was for transportation of sal biz project. the permit presented was transport permit form no- whereas for transportation of kendu leaves from one godown to other godown, transport permit form no- is used. in the permit presented by the driver no unit was mentioned with respect to kendu leaves though it is necessary to mention unit, in the transportation permit form of the kendu leaves. the further case of prosecution is that when authorities were satisfied that kendu leaves loaded on the truck no. bhm is illegal then truck diver anwar hussain an khalasi karu mahto were taken in custody and truck no. bhm along with kendu leaves were seized under of bihar kendu leavescontrol of trade act,. the seizure list was prepared at the place of occurrence itself.

state of nct of delhi vs sanjay on september, . criminal no. of arose out of an order passed by the delhi high court on an application under cr.p.c. seeking quashing of the fir registered at police station alipur under sections 120b of the mines and mineral development and regulation act, in short the mmdr act. . apart from threatening bridges, sand mining transforms the riverbeds into large and deep pits; as a result, the groundwater table drops leaving the drinking water wells on the embankments of these rivers dry. bed degradation from in-stream mining lowers the elevation of stream flow and the floodplain water table, which in turn, can eliminate water table-dependent woody vegetation in riparian areas and decrease wetted periods in riparian wetlands.

firstly, the seventy-two miniature paintings, the sixty-eight catalogs, and the eighteen photographs allegedly recovered from the appellants home were never sealed on the spot. secondly, there is no site plan of the place from where the objects mentioned above were recovered. the rajasthan police rules the police rules, for short are emphatic on the point that when a recovery is to be made, when objects are discovered, they should be sealed and marked at the place of recovery itself. . of course, the prosecution has examined ram babu vijay p. w. the photographerin order to prove the recovery. however, even his testimony does not rush to the rescue of the prosecution. firstly, in his statement under cr. p. c. ex. d . he claims that the photos were taken from an ordinary camera which uses a film. but in his testimony he claims that he had used a digital camera for photographing the half-burned and unburned photographs and paintings and for photographing the broken door. when the contradiction is pointed out to him, he denies that he had informed the police in his statement that he had used an ordinary camera. thus, the witness shifts his stand. secondly, he claims that he had made a cd of the six photographs which were clicked by him. yet, the prosecution has not produced any such cd during the trial.

sc no. state vs . kailash prajapati on april, . it was a winter morning in delhi when the husband of this lady, i.e. complainant, was out of station, and she came back after dropping her children to the school bus at around am and found that her expensive wrist watch and rs., which she had kept on telephone table are missing. she made inquiry from her domestic help i.e. accused herein, who feigned the sc no. state vs. kailash prajapati ignorance by saying main nahi jaanta. but it is very difficult to conceal the truth. the strap of the watch was seen hanging out of pocket of the suspect. on further inquiry by the lady, the suspect again tried to divert the issue and went towards another room. suddenly, this lady found a nylon rope around her neck and to her horror it was none else than the same servant who had put a noose around her neck and was pulling it from the back. obviously the lady could die. the servant did not stop here. he also removed the bangles from her right hand and started pulling the rope very hard. he was shouting that he would not spare the lady. only a hand of the victim between noose and neck was saving her from strangulation. offender was trying to remove her hand and was continuously beating her. the offender had become very cruel at that time. he dragged the lady by pulling her hair, giving a punch on her face and tried to gag her. somehow the lady had a hard bite on the finger of the offender, which resulted into the bleeding from the hand of accused and his grip became loose. victim ran towards the main door.

lal chand sharma vs state of haryana and anr on august, ii that it is not even the case of the complainant that he had paid the entire sale consideration agreed upon between the parties. rather the prime grievance of the complainant was that after receipt of part payment of the sale consideration, the petitioner had failed to perform his part of the agreement. therefore, in the circumstances, at best the case of the complainant against the petitioner could be one of subsequent breach of contract and not a case of cheating under of neutral citation no::phhc: crm-m no. of om : phhc: annexure p3, there was neither any such condition stipulated therein nor any material to substantiate the allegations levelled by the complainant. vi that further in the fir, neither were there any allegations qua clubbing nor forgery levelled, and they had been raised by the complainant only in his reply. in the challan submitted by the police annexure p-, a categoric finding had been given by the investigating agency that no offence under ipc was made out which stood corroborated by the report received from the forensic science laboratory. there was no shred of evidence to show the commission of the offences alleged and it was thus obvious that the criminal proceedings, which had been launched by the complainant were on the face of it, vexatious, instituted only with an ulterior motive, to wreak vengeance upon the petitioner.

state through cbi v. bibianus toppo ors vs state on february, iii it was alleged that accused vir singh a11 had applied for a passport in the fictitious name of rajender kumar kapoor in the year . accordingly, the said application was processed in the file no. b- dated november , . iv it was alleged that verification of identity, antecedents, characters and residence of the applicant was done by asi umrao singh, special branch, delhi a7. it was alleged that he had submitted a false verification report without verifying the identity, antecedents, character and address of the applicant. v it was alleged that on the basis of his false report, the passport bearing no. b- was issued on december , to vir singh a11 in the name of rajender kumar kapoor. vii it was alleged that harish kumar malhotra a10 had facilitated a11 in obtaining passport in the fictitious name as he had forged the signature of two persons, namely, raj kumar saini and yogender singh on the witness identification slip in the presence of asi umrao singh. it was alleged the handwriting expert in his report categorical stated that a10 had forged the signature of the above said two persons. it was further alleged that a10 had also obtained the said passport. viii it was further alleged that on the basis of said original passport, multiple applications were made in the office of rpo, new delhi on august , , december , , february , , february , , march , and april , for the issuance of additional passport booklets with the personal particulars and address of rajender kumar kapoor, but with different photographs and signatures on each occasion.

delhi high court m.s. lamba vs state cbi on march, equivalent citations: 1995iiaddelhi, drj58 judgment vijender jain, j. this is a petition for setting aside the order dated and for quashing charge dated framed under sec. 120b indian penal code read with sec. indian penal code passed by metropolitan magistrate, new delhi. mr.mathur, learned senior counsel for the petitioner at the outset has contended that r.c. dli on the basis of which fir was filed was under sec.120b indian penal code read with sec. read with sec.d of the prevention of corruption act hereinafter referred as the act and no investigation was conducted by the respondent under sec. indian penal code mr.mathur has contended that if the fir was under the provisions of prevention of corruption act the forum for such trial is before a special judge appointed under the act and prosecution cannot camouflage by choosing not to file and pursue the complaint under the provisions of the act and file the same under sec. indian penal code in support of his arguments he has cited basir-ul-haq and others vs. the state of west bengal reads as under:- though,in our judgment, s. does not bar the trial of an accused person for a distinct offence disclosed by the same facts and which is not included within the ambit of that section, it has also to be borne in mind that the provisions of that section cannot be evaded by resorting to devices or camouflages. the test whether there is evasion of the section or not is whether the facts disclose primarily and essentially an offence for which a complaint of the court or of the public servant is required.

state vs radhakrishnan on february, . according to the petitioner herein, the learned sessions judge has failed to note that the facts of the case would squarely attract the provision of money circulation scheme which is banned under the prize chits and money circulation schemes banning act, central act and the case does fall under the category of cheating under i.p.c. and the first information report prima facie discloses the offences alleged and the sessions judge has failed to take note of the gravity of the offences and the possibility of tampering of witnesses by the respondent. it is further stated in the petition that the respondent herein is the main accused in the case and the money circulation scheme promoted by him is opposed to public policy and has been banned and it is further stated that more than one lakh of persons have been cheated to the tune of more than rs. crores. the petitioner has further stated that custodial interrogation of the respondent is very much essential for the recovery of the money that might have been siphoned of and if it is not taken, the investigation would be seriously hampered and prejudiced. it is further stated that the learned sessions judge considered matters which are not relevant while exercising the discretion in granting anticipatory bail and he has failed to take note the law laid down by the supreme court in this regard and hence the grant of anticipatory bail is liable to be cancelled. . the respondentaccused filed counter stating that the offences alleged against the respondent except under i.p.c. are all bailable and the contents of first information report does not prima facie make out a case under i.p.c.

rajeev kumar goyal raj kumar goyal vs state through c.b.i. on august, . at the time of admission of s on st march , this court suspended the s awarded to each of the appellants during the pendency of the s subject to terms. the court s informed that the fine amounts have already been paid by the appellants. . before discussing the particular facts of these cases it is necessary to understand how the marine open policy comes to be issued. when goods are transported from one destination to another, the goods are insured for the period during which they are being transported and till they are delivered at the destination. the consignor is usually the person selling the goods and the consignee is the purchaser of the goods. the premium could be paid for, depending on the terms of purchase, by either the consignor or the consignee. the marine open policy covers internal transport of goods by road as well. a cover note is issued by a clerk in the insurance company who is authorised to do so. at that stage the insurer usually submits the proposal and requisite documents. after collection of the premium amount, the policy cover note is issued. when goods are either stolen during transit or damaged or failed to be delivered, a claim is lodged with the branch which issued the policy. the insured gives intimation to the nic and the branch manager appoints a surveyor, from the approved list for that branch, to assess the loss. the surveyor then submits a report along with photographs, the proposal certificate and the goods received. a clerk in the concerned nic branch then processes the claim and submits it to the branch manager for approval.

deepak handa vs c.b.i. on august, . at the time of admission of s on st march , this court suspended the s awarded to each of the appellants during the pendency of the s subject to terms. the court s informed that the fine amounts have already been paid by the appellants. . before discussing the particular facts of these cases it is necessary to understand how the marine open policy comes to be issued. when goods are transported from one destination to another, the goods are insured for the period during which they are being transported and till they are delivered at the destination. the consignor is usually the person selling the goods and the consignee is the purchaser of the goods. the premium could be paid for, depending on the terms of purchase, by either the consignor or the consignee. the marine open policy covers internal transport of goods by road as well. a cover note is issued by a clerk in the insurance company who is authorised to do so. at that stage the insurer usually submits the proposal and requisite documents. after collection of the premium amount, the policy cover note is issued. when goods are either stolen during transit or damaged or failed to be delivered, a claim is lodged with the branch which issued the policy. the insured gives intimation to the nic and the branch manager appoints a surveyor, from the approved list for that branch, to assess the loss. the surveyor then submits a report along with photographs, the proposal certificate and the goods received. a clerk in the concerned nic branch then processes the claim and submits it to the branch manager for approval.

state vs chander shekhar sharma. on may, . kidnapping and rape are dark realities in indian society like in any other nation. this abnormal conduct is rooted in physical force as well as familiar and other power which the abuser uses to pressure his victim. nor is abuse by known and unknown persons confined to a single political ideology or to one economic system. it transcends barriers of age, class, language, caste, community, sex and even family. the only commonality is power which triggers and feeds rape. disbelief, denial and cover-up to preserve the family reputation are often then placed above the interests of the victim and her abuse. reliance on the material on the internet. rape is an abominable and ghastly and it worsens and becomes inhuman and barbaric when the accused is known to the victim, as in the present case being her neighbour, who is allegedly subjected to unwanted physical contact by a perverted male. . courts are expected to show great responsibility while trying an accused on charges of rape. they must deal with such cases with utmost sensitivity. the courts should examine the broader probabilities of a case sessions case number : of . . after completion of the investigation, the charge sheet was filed before the court of the learned metropolitan magistrate on and after its committal, the case was assigned to the court of sessions case number : of . pw-b was made on the basis of which the fir ex. pw-a was lodged and the certificate under evidence act ex.pw1a1 was issued. endorsement on the rukka ex.pw1b was made. the prosecutrix was taken by whc.

state vs shankar kumar and another. -:: page of . on january, . kidnapping and rape are dark realities in indian society like in any other nation. per cent of our children are sexually abused, according to a statistic from a survey done by the government of india. this abnormal conduct is rooted in physical force as well as familiar and other power which the abuser uses to pressure his victim. nor is abuse by known and unknown persons confined to a single political ideology or to one economic system. it transcends barriers of age, class, language, caste, community, sex and even family. the only commonality is power which triggers and feeds rape. disbelief, denial and cover-up to preserve the family reputation are often then placed above the interests of the victim and her abuse. reliance on the material on the internet. rape is an abominable and ghastly and it worsens and becomes inhuman and barbaric when the victim is a minor female child, as in the present case, who is allegedly subjected to unwanted physical contact by a perverted male adult. fir no. , police station moti nagar, under sections , , and of the indian penal code. state versus shankar kumar and another. -:: page of ::- -:: ::- an accused on charges of rape. they must deal with such cases with utmost sensitivity. the courts should examine the broader probabilities of a case and not get swayed by minor contradictions or insignificant discrepancies in the statement of the witnesses, which are not of a fatal nature to throw out allegations of rape. this is all the more important because of lately crime against women in general and rape in particular is on the increase.

state vs brahm dev kamat and another. -:: page of . on march, . kidnapping and rape are dark realities in indian society like in any other nation. per cent of our children are sexually abused, according to a statistic from a survey done by the government of india. this abnormal conduct is rooted in physical force as well as familiar and other power which the abuser uses to pressure his victim. nor is abuse by known and unknown persons confined to a single political ideology or to one economic system. it transcends barriers of age, class, language, caste, community, sex and even family. the only commonality is power which triggers and feeds rape. disbelief, denial and cover-up to preserve the family reputation are often then placed above the interests of the victim and her abuse. reliance on the material on the internet. rape is an abominable and ghastly and it worsens and becomes inhuman and barbaric when the victim is a young woman, as in the present case, who is allegedly subjected to unwanted physical contact by a perverted male adult. . courts are expected to show great responsibility while trying an accused on charges of rape. they must deal with such cases with utmost sensitivity. the courts should examine the broader probabilities of a case sessions case number : of . . mr. brahm dev kamat and mr. om prakash, both the accused persons, have been charge sheeted by police station punjabi bagh, delhi for the offence under sections of the indian penal code hereinafter referred to as the .. at h.no.

x vs state nct of delhi and anr on january, . the case reveals the story of matrimonial discord between two couples and the complications that arose inviting criminality when two different partners of the story fell in love with each other and decided to marry again. the uniqueness of the story is that ms. x was married to mr. a and respondent no. mr. singh was married to ms. b. the complaint reveals that ms. x was known to mr. singh prior to her marriage to a and mr. singhs marriage to ms. b since the year . their relationship did not culminate into marriage and they were married to different partners in the year itself. they had parted ways and had become distant not only in terms of any relationship but also became distant in terms of the countries they chose to live in. petitioner, ms. x settled in india after marriage with mr. a and respondent no. settled in canada after marriage with ms. b. unfortunately, their matrimonial lives were unsettled soon and both as per the investigation and complaint were carrying the burden of unhappy marriage with their respective partners. . the twist in the story was that the mutual unhappiness experienced by ms. x and mr. singh in their marriage with their respective partners brought about mutual love between them despite still being legally married to their respective partners. . ms. x and her husband mr. a mutually decided to separate from each other and after extensive deliberations, both the families collectively decided that after obtaining divorce, the petitioner ms. x would move to canada with her children, since her family including her father and brother were planning to settle in canada. . during this time i.e.

dr. ashok kumar agrawal vs state of u.p. others on august, . brief facts of the case are that :- . a complaint annexure to the affidavit has been filed by amarjeet singh opposite party no. hereinafter referred to as o.p. no. in the court of chief judicial magistrate, saharanpur under , x of act, against the applicant stating therein that the complainant is chamar by caste which comes under scheduled caste. the applicant is agrawal, belongs to the upper caste. .. the applicant had a clinic at moreganj, opposite court, court road, saharanpur. . the complaint was engaged in that clinic since as a receptionist. it was agreed that he would be paid a consolidated salary of rs.-. however, he was paid rs.- and sometime - only and was being informed that remaining amount of his salary would be paid to him subsequently but the remaining amount of salary was never paid to him. . though the complainant left the job of the applicant on , yet he often visited to the applicants clinic, in connection of experience certificate for seeking job elsewhere. . on he was threatened by some hired goondas at behest of the applicant and was coerced to sign on a blank paper. . on , the complainant was again abused. . considering the allegations of complaint as well as statements of complainant and his witnesses recorded under and cr.p.c. respectively, alongwih other evidence available on record, the court below found prima facie case against the applicant and summoned him vide order dated annexure to the affidavit to face trial under x of act, . .

on or before , the accused had caused disappearance of documents of the vehicle of the deceased and committee register with intention to screen himself from legal punishment. the entire case of the prosecution in this regard is based on the supplementary disclosure statement of accused himself which is proved as ex.pw15e. however, disclosure statement of an accused is not an admissible evidence which can be read against him. in addition to this, the case of the prosecution regarding the possession of documents of vehicle of deceased by the accused is highly doubtful. firstly, it is amply clear on record that vehicle bearing no. dl-1lt- was not in the name of the deceased but his brother sandeep vashishth. secondly, as per suicide note ex.p-, the documents of the vehicle and its keys were in possession of accused, whereas as per the testimony of pw1, the documents, vehicle as well as its keys were snatched by the accused which were returned back as informed by accused to him on , later on in september , the key and the vehicle were again snatched by accused from the possession of his deceased brother. in contradistinction, pw2 stated that her deceased husband informed her on that accused had snatched the documents, keys and the vehicle from him. pw3 on the other hand stated that documents of the said vehicle and keys of the same were in the custody of the accused. none of the witnesses pw1, pw2 and pw3 are the witnesses to the taking away of the documents of the vehicle or its keys from the deceased by the accused. they are also contradicting the suicide note to the effect that even vehicle in question was snatched by the accused from the deceased.
